ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese chive and egg pancakes; 'pancake food product' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for savoury Asian-style pancakes is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pancakes and crepes) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Chinese chive and egg pancakes in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chinese chive and egg pancakes or a sufficiently close savoury pancake analogue in the clinical terminology as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium Chinese chive and egg pancake weighing approximately 100 g, roughly the size of a small dinner plate.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Chive And Egg Pancakes — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Chinese Chive And Egg Pancakes' (cr_id G00010083). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this canonical record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese chive and egg pancakes. GI estimated at 67 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: wheat-flour pancakes made from refined white flour, which are reported at GI ~67 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The dish is predominantly refined wheat flour (approximately 90% of carbohydrate contribution) with minimal fibre (0.7 g/100g), supporting this analogy. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100 = 67 × 45.38 / 100 ≈ 30.4.

hypertension.guidance: Chinese chive and egg pancakes contain only 5 mg sodium per 100 g, making the dish itself a very low sodium food; however, patients with hypertension should avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chilli sauce, which can substantially increase total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (45.4 g/100g) with very low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) producing a medium-to-high glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high refined carbohydrate content and low fibre of this dish result in a glycaemic load of approximately 30 per 100 g serving, which is clinically significant for individuals with type 2 diabetes; portion size should be limited to half a pancake and paired with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to attenuate postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.38 g/100g) and negligible cholesterol (0 mg/100g) with low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The extremely low fat and cholesterol content of Chinese chive and egg pancakes presents minimal direct cardiovascular risk from the dish itself; however, the high refined carbohydrate content may contribute to postprandial triglyceride elevation, so patients with dyslipidaemia should moderate portion size and prefer wholemeal flour variants.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory organosulfur compounds from Chinese chives; very low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chinese chives provide organosulfur compounds with anti-inflammatory properties, and the very low fat content minimises pro-inflammatory lipid exposure; patients should prefer wholemeal flour to reduce the glycaemic-driven inflammatory burden associated with the high refined carbohydrate content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Chive and Egg Pancakes (韭菜鸡蛋饼, Jiǔcài Jīdàn Bǐng) are a popular savoury pan-fried pancake originating from Chinese cuisine and widely consumed in Singapore as a home-cooked meal or hawker-style dish. The pancakes are made by combining a batter of wheat flour and water with beaten eggs and finely chopped Chinese chives (garlic chives, Allium tuberosum), then pan-frying in a small amount of vegetable oil until golden and slightly crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. The flavour profile is mildly savoury with a distinctive grassy, garlicky note from the chives and a subtle eggy richness. Per 100 g, the dish provides 207 kcal, 4.05 g protein, 0.38 g fat, and 45.38 g carbohydrate with only 0.7 g dietary fibre and 5 mg sodium. The very low fat and sodium content make it a heart-friendly base dish, but the high refined carbohydrate load and low fibre produce a medium glycaemic index of approximately 67 and a glycaemic load of ~30 per 100 g, which is clinically significant for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. Iron content is notable at 2.32 mg/100g, partly contributed by the chives. The dish is vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, though it contains gluten and egg allergens. It is commonly served with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, or chilli oil, which can significantly increase sodium intake and should be used sparingly by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010083), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The input record carries a confidence of 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. The source match is exact for this dish.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Chinese chive and egg pancakes is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this item as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish; no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match to justify imputation. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the approved source (HPB Foodo G00010083) — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. 4. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat-flour pancakes from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in any approved source. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish in current releases.
